title: The Compression Problem
concept: Encoder-decoder models compress input text into one fixed-size vector, creating a bottleneck that erases details on long sequences.

exercises.prompt: Which type of model takes an input sequence and translates it into an output sequence?
exercises.explain: An encoder-decoder model is a neural network design commonly used for translation, where one part processes the input (encoder) and another generates the output (decoder).

cards.title: Squeezing Too Hard
cards.body: Have you ever tried to summarize a whole book in just one sentence? You can capture the main plot, but you lose all the magic details. Early AI translation models faced this exact struggle!

cards.title: What is the Compression Problem?
cards.body: The compression problem (or information bottleneck) happens when an encoder-decoder model squeezes an entire input sequence into a single, fixed-length vector. Because this vector has a limited size, it cannot hold all the rich details of very long sentences.

cards.title: The Post-It Note Bottleneck
cards.body: Imagine trying to copy a massive, detailed painting onto a tiny post-it note, and then asking a friend to repaint the original using only that post-it. The encoder is forced to squeeze everything into one tiny vector space, causing crucial details to get lost.

cards.title: Lost in Translation
cards.body: If you feed a 100-word paragraph into a basic encoder, it compresses the whole thing into one list of numbers. When the decoder tries to translate it, it might forget the specific adjectives or names mentioned at the very beginning of the paragraph.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Models Fix Everything
cards.body: Many think that building a larger model automatically solves this. But no matter how big the model is, as long as it relies on a single fixed-size vector to pass information between the encoder and decoder, long inputs will always suffer from information loss.

cards.title: Compression vs. Attention
cards.body: See how compressing information into a single vector compares to letting the model look at all words directly.

left.text: Forces all words into one tight space. Long texts lose crucial details.

right.text: Allows the model to look back at specific individual words as needed.

exercises.prompt: What happens to a basic encoder-decoder model's performance as the input sentence gets much longer?

options.text: The model gets smarter and learns faster.
The model starts to forget details and translation quality drops.
The model refuses to process any text at all.
The model automatically splits the sentence into ten pieces.

exercises.explain: As the input sentence gets longer, a single fixed-size vector runs out of capacity to store all the details, causing the model's performance to drop.

exercises.prompt: Traditional encoder-decoder models represent the entire input text as a single, fixed-size vector.
exercises.explain: True. In a basic encoder-decoder setup, the encoder compresses the entire input sequence into a single vector of fixed size before handing it over to the decoder.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe this AI compression challenge:
exercises.sentence: Forcing a long text sequence into a single fixed-size vector creates an information ________ that discards fine details.
exercises.explain: Squeezing a long sentence into one fixed-size vector creates an information bottleneck, where detailed context is squeezed out and lost.

exercises.prompt:
Input: 'The girl wearing a red hat walked to the park.'
Model A: 'A girl walked to the park.'
Model B: 'The girl with the red hat walked to the park.'
Which statement is true?

options.text: Model A is much better because it made the sentence shorter.
Model A suffers from the compression problem, losing the word 'red'.
Model B is broken because it kept too many words.

exercises.explain: The model using a single fixed-size vector is suffering from the compression bottleneck, leading to missing details like the color. The second model likely uses attention to keep track of individual words.

exercises.prompt: Why does the 'Compression Problem' occur in traditional encoder-decoder neural networks?

options.text: It makes the model process words too fast, skipping vocabulary terms.
It forces the entire input into a single fixed-size vector, losing details.
It allows the decoder to look back at every individual word in the input.
It makes the model run out of computer memory before training starts.

exercises.explain: Standard encoder-decoder models force the entire input sequence into a single, fixed-size vector. This creates a severe information bottleneck for long sentences because the vector cannot hold all the fine-grained details, leading to poorer translations or summaries.
